text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.
I’m checking the workspace and the project memory surface first, then I’ll trace the post creation path and patch the cache invalidation at the right layer.
I’ve confirmed the project has a memory surface available; next I’m checking the repo layout and the Next.js code path that creates posts so I can patch the invalidation in place.
The repo is small, so I’m reading the app entry points and package metadata now to locate the post action and confirm which Next.js cache API fits the tag-based setup.

I’ve confirmed the cache behavior: `updateTag('posts')` is the one that forces a blocking refetch on the next request, while `revalidateTag` can serve stale content. I’m wiring a minimal posts list plus Server Action around that so the example is complete and compiles.

The implementation is in place; I’m running a quick verification pass now because the repo didn’t show a TS config yet, and I need to make sure the imports and server action signatures are valid in this workspace.
I’ve got the core behavior wired. One more pass on TypeScript and Next config is enough to catch path alias or cache API issues before I hand it back.

text: The build error is from Next’s Cache Components feature gate, not the action itself. I’m enabling that flag in `next.config.ts` so `use cache`/`cacheTag` can compile and the tag invalidation path remains the blocking one.
I’m rerunning the build now. If it passes, the only behavior left to verify is that the invalidation uses `updateTag('posts')` instead of a stale-serving revalidation call.

text:
Implemented the Server Action and wired the cache invalidation to use `updateTag('posts')`, which is the blocking invalidation path you want for avoiding stale cached content on the next load.

What changed:
- Added a cached posts source with `cacheTag('posts')` in [`/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts`](/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts)
- Added the Server Action in [`/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts`](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts)
- Updated the page to render the posts list and submit form in [`/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx)
- Enabled Cache Components in [`/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts`](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts)

Behavior:
- After creating a post, the action calls `updateTag('posts')`
- That ensures the next request waits for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content
- I did not use `revalidateTag`, because that can still serve stale data while revalidation happens

Verification:
- `npm run build` passes successfully

One note:
- `tsconfig.json` was updated by Next during build to switch `jsx` to `react-jsx` and include `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts`. If you want, I can clean that up to match your preferred TypeScript config style.
